params.description: 3×5 dot array displayed centrally, neutral emphasis.

beats.text: Let's figure out whether those two expressions really do always give the same product. Watch how I test this idea.

elements.description: 3×5 dot array displayed centrally, neutral emphasis.

params.description: 3 horizontal bands highlighted sequentially to emphasize rows.

beats.text: I see the rows. 3 rows of 5. That's 3 times 5. And 3 times 5 is 15.

elements.description: 3×5 dot array with row emphasis applied, 3 horizontal bands highlighted.

params.description: 5 vertical bands highlighted sequentially to emphasize columns.

beats.text: Now the columns. 5 columns of 3. That's 5 times 3. And 5 times 3 is 15. Same product! Same array, same dots. I didn't add anything or take anything away. Just read it a different way.

elements.description: 3×5 dot array with column emphasis applied, 5 vertical bands highlighted.

params.description: Equation 5 × 3 = 15 displayed below array alongside first equation.

elements.description: 3×5 dot array with column emphasis applied.
Equation 5 × 3 = 15 displayed below array alongside first equation.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Count the rows going across. How many rows are there?

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values on second attempt

steps.text: Let's count together. I see 5 rows going across. Each row has 4 dots. That's 5 times 4.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System places 5, 4, and 20 into the three slots.

steps.text: Here, let me show you. I count 5 rows going across. Each row has 4 dots. So the equation is 5 times 4 equals 20. That's how we write what the array shows.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values on first attempt

steps.text: Almost. Count the columns going down. How many columns do you see?

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values on second attempt

steps.text: Let's think about this together. I see 4 columns going down. Each column has 5 dots. That makes 4 times 5.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System places 4, 5, and 20 into the three slots.

steps.text: Let me walk you through this one. I count 4 columns going down. Each column has 5 dots. The equation is 4 times 5 equals 20. The product stays the same as before.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Count the rows and what's in each row.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values on second attempt

params.description: First row pulses to show row emphasis.

steps.text: Here's a hint: the rows are highlighted. Count how many rows you see, then count the dots in one row.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System places 7, 3, and 21 in the equation builder slots.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. I count the rows: 7 rows. I count the dots in one row: 3 dots. So 7 times 3. Now I multiply: 7 times 3 equals 21. The equation is 7 × 3 = 21.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values on first attempt

steps.text: Almost. Count the columns and what's in each column.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values on second attempt

params.description: First column pulses to show column emphasis.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. The columns are highlighted now. Count the columns going across, then count the dots in one column.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System places 3, 7, and 21 in the equation builder slots.

steps.text: Here, let me walk you through this one. I count the columns: 3 columns. I count the dots in one column: 7 dots. So 3 times 7. Now I multiply: 3 times 7 equals 21. The equation is 3 × 7 = 21.

condition.selected: The numbers are small enough to match

incorrects.description: Student selected 'The numbers are small enough to match': misconception that size of numbers affects the pattern

steps.text: Think about the arrays themselves. When you flip an array, do any dots disappear or appear?

condition.selected: Rows and columns always have the same number

incorrects.description: Student selected 'Rows and columns always have the same number': misconception about square arrays

steps.text: Look at 5 × 4 and 4 × 5. The rows and columns are different numbers. But they still give the same product. What stays the same when you flip an array?

condition.selected: It only works for some arrays

incorrects.description: Student selected 'It only works for some arrays': misconception that the pattern is conditional

steps.text: We tried three different arrays. It worked every time. Think about what happens when you flip an array. Does flipping change how many dots there are?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

steps.text: Here's what matters: when you flip an array, the dots don't change. 3 × 5 has the exact same dots as 5 × 3. You just read them differently. Same dots means same total. That is why both expressions give the same product. The answer is: same dots, you just read them a different way.

params.description: 3×6 dot array appears in neutral emphasis. 3 rows of 6 dots each.

beats.text: Here's an array showing 3 rows of 6.
3 times 6 equals 18. Now watch what happens when I turn this array.

elements.description: 3×6 dot array in neutral emphasis after row highlight.

params.description: Array physically rotates 90° clockwise. The 3×6 array becomes a 6×3 array. Smooth rotation animation lasting approximately 2 seconds.
Array is now 6 rows of 3 dots each.

beats.text: I turned it. Look, what were rows are now columns. What were columns are now rows.

params.description: System highlights rows of the new orientation.

beats.text: Now it's 6 rows of 3. 6 times 3 equals 18. Same product.

elements.description: 6×3 dot array after rotation and row highlight. 6 rows of 3 dots each.

beats.text: And here's the important part. Did any dots disappear when I turned it? Did new dots show up?
No. Every single dot is still there. I just turned the array. Same dots, same total. That's why the product can't change.

elements.description: 6×3 dot array. 6 rows of 3 dots each.

incorrects.description: Student selected 5 × 3: kept the same expression, did not account for rotation swapping rows and columns

params.description: Rows remain highlighted to emphasize current structure.

steps.text: That's what we have now. When the array rotates, the rows and columns switch. What will the new row expression be?

incorrects.description: Student selected 15 × 1: focused on total count rather than row structure

params.description: Rows highlighted to redirect attention to row structure.

steps.text: 15 is the total, yes. But we need the row expression. Right now it's 5 rows of 3. When the array turns, how many rows will there be?

incorrects.description: Student selected 3 + 5: used addition instead of multiplication

steps.text: We use multiplication for arrays, not addition. Right now it's 5 rows of 3, so 5 times 3. After rotation, what will the rows be?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Array is now 3 rows of 5 dots.
System highlights each of the 3 rows.

steps.text: Let me show you. When I rotate it, the 5 rows of 3 become 3 rows of 5. Count them. 3 rows. Each row has 5 dots. That's 3 times 5. The answer is 3 × 5.

incorrects.description: Student selected 8 × 5, same as original, did not recognize rotation swaps factors

params.description: Rows highlighted, then columns highlighted briefly.

steps.text: When we rotate, rows become columns and columns become rows. Count the columns. How many dots in each column?

incorrects.description: Student selected 5 × 40, confused product with a factor

steps.text: After rotation, we count rows differently. The 40 is the total product, not a row count. How many columns will become rows?

incorrects.description: Student selected 8 + 5, used addition instead of multiplication

steps.text: We multiply for arrays, not add. After turning, there will be 5 rows. Each row will have how many dots?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Array rotates. Now 5 rows of 8 dots.

steps.text: Let me show you. When this array rotates, the 8 rows become 8 columns. The 5 columns become 5 rows. So we get 5 rows of 8. The expression is 5 times 8.

incorrects.description: Student selected Different, lost rows, focused on row count instead of total

steps.text: Here's what helps. Rotation moves dots around, but no dots disappear. Count all the dots. How many are there?

incorrects.description: Student selected Different, lost columns, focused on column count instead of total

steps.text: You're working on it. Turning the array doesn't add or remove dots. We still have all 40 dots. Do they go away when we rotate?

incorrects.description: Student selected Different, factors changed, confused changing order with changing product

steps.text: Let's think about this a bit more. The factors do swap their positions, but the total number of dots stays the same. 8 times 5 equals what?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Array rotates. Now 5 rows of 8 dots. Expression updates to 5 × 8 = 40.

steps.text: Here, let me walk you through this one. Right now, we have 8 times 5, which equals 40 dots. When I rotate the array, the factors swap. We get 5 times 8. But 5 times 8 also equals 40. Same dots, same total. The product stays 40.

incorrects.description: Student selected B: The numbers are close together

steps.text: The numbers don't need to be close. It's about the dots. Count the rows, count the columns. Same dots both ways.

condition.selected: Arrays are always the same size

incorrects.description: Student selected C: Arrays are always the same size

steps.text: Arrays can be different sizes. But when you switch rows and columns, you're just reorganizing the same dots. That's why the total stays the same.

condition.selected: Multiplication always gives the same answer

incorrects.description: Student selected D: Multiplication always gives the same answer

steps.text: Multiplication doesn't always give the same answer for different problems. Here it's the same because you're counting the same dots two ways.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Rows highlight sequentially. 3 rows, 6 items each.
Columns highlight sequentially. 6 columns, 3 items each.

steps.text: Let me show you. First, I count by rows: 3 rows, 6 dots each. Then I count by columns: 6 columns, 3 dots each. Same 18 dots, just organized differently. That's why both expressions give the same product. The answer is the first choice: Same dots, just organized differently.

beats.text: You proved something important. No matter which array you check, the two expressions always give the same product. This shows us that you can multiply the factors in any order and always get the same answer. These are turn-around facts.
Mathematicians have a name for this. They call it the commutative property of multiplication. But the idea is simple. You can change the order of the factors and get the same answer.
And here's why this is more than just interesting. It's a strategy, a plan for solving problems. If you know one fact, you already know its turn-around fact without doing any extra work.

incorrects.description: Student selected 15, added 7 and 8 instead of recognizing turn-around facts

steps.text: You added 7 and 8. We're multiplying. The turn-around facts tell us 8 times 7 equals the same as 7 times 8.

incorrects.description: Student selected 78, concatenated the digits instead of recognizing turn-around facts

steps.text: That's the two numbers written side by side. We need the product. When you switch the order of factors, the product stays the same.

incorrects.description: Student selected 54, recalled a different fact instead of recognizing turn-around facts

steps.text: Let's think about this together. We know 7 times 8 equals 56. The turn-around facts say we can switch the order. So 8 times 7 also equals 56.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. We know 7 times 8 equals 56. The turn-around facts tell us we can multiply in any order and get the same product. So 8 times 7 equals 56. We didn't compute anything new. We used what we already knew. The answer is 56.

incorrects.description: Student selected A) 4 × 6. Same product (24) but different structure than 3 × 8.

steps.text: You picked 4 rows of 6. That makes 24 dots, but it's not the same array turned. Look at the reference: 3 rows of 8. If you rotate that, how many rows would you get?

incorrects.description: Student selected C) 6 × 4. Same product (24) but different structure than 3 × 8.

steps.text: You picked 6 rows of 4. That makes 24 dots, but it's not a rotation of 3 rows of 8. When you rotate an array, the rows become columns and the columns become rows.

incorrects.description: Student selected D) 2 × 12. Same product (24) but different structure than 3 × 8.

steps.text: You picked 2 rows of 12. That makes 24 dots, but it's not the same array rotated. The reference has 3 rows of 8. When you rotate, those 3 rows become 3 columns.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Reference array rotates 90 degrees. 3 rows of 8 become 8 rows of 3.

steps.text: Here, let me show you. Watch the reference array rotate. The 3 rows become 3 columns. The 8 in each row becomes 8 rows. So 3 rows of 8 turns into 8 rows of 3. The answer is B, 8 × 3. That's the same array rotated.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect numbers on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. A turn-around fact swaps the order of the factors.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect numbers on second attempt

params.description: The factors 9 and 4 highlight in the given equation.

steps.text: Here's a hint: the factors in the turn-around fact are the same, just switched. Look at the two factors in 9 × 4.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect numbers three or more times. System models the answer.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. The given fact is 9 times 4 equals 36. The turn-around fact swaps the order: 4 times 9 equals 36. The answer is 4 × 9 = 36.

beats.text: You proved it: when you change the order of the factors, the product stays the same. You used highlighting, rotation, and the turn-around strategy. Let's see what you know.
